title: Stroke play vs match play: two different games
microFact: Stroke play counts every stroke over 18 holes. Match play is hole-by-hole combat — you can make a 9 and lose only one hole.
lesson:
The two mother formats:

STROKE PLAY (medal play): total your strokes for 18 holes; lowest total wins. Every stroke counts, disasters compound, and you must hole out everything. Most club competitions and all professional majors use it.

MATCH PLAY: you versus an opponent, hole by hole. Win a hole by scoring lower on it; the match score reads like "2 up" or "3&2" (3 holes up with 2 to play — match over). A blow-up 9 loses you exactly one hole, same as losing by one stroke. You can CONCEDE putts, holes, or the match.

Strategy differs completely: stroke play rewards avoiding disasters; match play rewards pressure and aggression when your opponent is in trouble.

The language you'll hear: "all square" (tied), "dormie" (up by exactly the holes remaining — can't lose in regulation), "1 down with three to play." The Ryder Cup is match play — which is why it produces golf's wildest theater.

quiz.question: A match ends '4&3'. What does that mean?
options: 4 holes up with 3 holes left to play
The winner shot 4 under on the back 3
quiz.explanation: 4 up with only 3 holes remaining — mathematically over, so the match ends there.
